narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your early academic deciles, LSAT score, family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, and it calculated a 73% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to pass and succeed in their programs. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, well below the average decile of about 5.5—something the model sees as a strong signal of challenges in sustaining performance leading up to the bar. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also a 1, again far lower than the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that often correlate with bar difficulties for students like you. Your undergraduate GPA provided the main counterbalance, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing since it's on par with successful applicants, but it wasn't strong enough to offset the early decile weaknesses. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your LSAT of 31 is notably below the 36.7 average, and your lower-middle family income bracket adds context the model weighs, though your full-time enrollment aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%. These patterns suggest the model flagged your profile as riskier than most, where stronger consistent deciles often tip the scales toward approval. While this decision stings, especially given your background and determination to pursue law, it doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your high school decile performance through targeted prep courses, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth since those early years. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could significantly shift the model's view in your favor. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
